form_title: Alabama Sales and Use Tax Return (My Alabama Taxes)

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total Alabama gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Total deductions (exempt sales)
lines.notes: Total exempt/deductible sales (wholesale/resale, exempt-org sales, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus deductions — the base for the 4.0% general state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (general rate 4.0%)
lines.notes: Alabama's general state rate is 4.0%. Reduced STATE rates apply to categories v1 does not split out — food/groceries (3.0% in 2025, scheduled to fall further), automotive vehicles (2.0%), and farm/manufacturing machinery (1.5%); report those at their reduced rates. Alabama also has extensive LOCAL (city/county) taxes, many self-administered: state and state-administered locals are filed via My Alabama Taxes / ONE SPOT, self-administered localities separately. v1 reports the 4.0% state portion only. A timely-filing discount on state sales tax is available — apply per the MAT instructions.

notes: Alabama files via My Alabama Taxes (MAT) / ONE SPOT (paper Form 2100 is retired). General state rate 4.0%. Reduced state rates (food 3.0%, automotive 2.0%, machinery 1.5%) and the many self-administered local jurisdictions are NOT split by v1 — it reports the 4.0% general state portion only; complete reduced-rate categories and local lines from your category/jurisdiction detail. A timely-filing vendor discount on state sales tax is available; apply per the MAT instructions.

form_title: Arkansas Excise (Sales and Use) Tax Return

lines.label: Gross receipts
lines.notes: Total gross receipts for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt receipts
lines.notes: Total exempt receipts (resale, prescription drugs, etc.). Food for home consumption is taxed at the reduced 0.125% state rate on a separate line.

lines.label: Net taxable
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 6.5% state rate.

lines.label: State tax (6.5%)
lines.notes: Arkansas state rate is 6.5%. County and city taxes are reported per jurisdiction on the local tax schedule at each jurisdiction's combined rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by AR county/city.

notes: AR files the Excise Tax Return (ET-1). State rate is 6.5%; county and city local taxes are reported per jurisdiction on the local schedule and are not split by v1. Food for home consumption is taxed at the reduced 0.125% state rate (a separate line); candy and soft drinks are at the full rate.

form_title: Arizona Transaction Privilege, Use, and Severance Tax Return

lines.label: Gross amount (retail)
lines.notes: Gross amount of retail business for the period (retail classification, business code 017), including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Deductions
lines.notes: Total deductions/exempt amounts (resale, exempt food for home consumption, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable
lines.notes: Gross minus deductions — the base for the 5.6% state retail TPT.

lines.label: State TPT, retail (5.6%)
lines.notes: Arizona state retail TPT rate is 5.6%. City and county TPT are reported per region/location code on the TPT-EZ at each jurisdiction's combined rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by AZ city/county, so complete the region lines from your location-level sales.

notes: AZ TPT is a transaction privilege (seller) tax reported by business class (retail = 017) and by region/location code on Form TPT-EZ (or TPT-2 for multi-location). State retail rate is 5.6%; city (1.5%-4%) and county (0.5%-1.1%) TPT are reported per region and are not split by v1. Arizona exempts food for home consumption from the state rate but many cities tax it. The accounting/vendor credit on TPT is small and tiered — not modeled in v1.

form_title: State, Local, and District Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Total sales
lines.notes: Gross sales for the quarter (round to whole dollars per the CDTFA-401-INST instruction at the top of the form).

lines.label: Purchases subject to use tax
lines.notes: Use-tax on out-of-state untaxed purchases — TaxLocus cannot infer from the sales ledger. Defaults to 0; override before filing if you owe.

lines.label: Total (line 1 plus line 2)
lines.notes: With line 2 defaulted to 0, equals gross sales.

lines.label: Total nontaxable transactions reported
lines.notes: From Section B, line 6. TaxLocus reports the total exempt amount from the ledger; Sections A and B on page 3 must be broken out by reason (resale, food, government, etc.) before filing.

lines.label: Transactions subject to tax
lines.notes: Line 3 minus line 11.

lines.label: State tax (6.00%)
lines.notes: Multiply line 12 by 0.0600.

lines.label: County tax (0.25%)
lines.notes: Multiply line 12 by 0.0025 — the statewide county portion.

lines.label: Local tax (1.00%)
lines.notes: Multiply line 12 by 0.01 — the statewide local Bradley-Burns portion.

lines.label: District tax
lines.notes: District-tax computation requires CDTFA-531-A2 (Schedule A2) — per-district allocations TaxLocus does not yet produce. Defaults to 0; complete Schedule A2 separately. Filers with no district-tax engagement leave this 0.

lines.label: Total state, county, local, and district tax
lines.notes: Sum of lines 13, 14, 15, 16. State + county + local = 7.25% (0.0600 + 0.0025 + 0.01). District tax (line 16) added separately.

notes: CDTFA-401-A is quarterly. Line 16 (district tax) requires CDTFA-531-A2 Schedule — TaxLocus defaults it to 0; merchants engaged in district-tax districts must complete the schedule. Sections A/B (deductions breakdown) on page 3 must be filled out before filing if line 11 > 0. v1 reports the total only; per-reason breakouts deferred.

form_title: Colorado Retail Sales Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales and services, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Deductions and exemptions
lines.notes: Total deductions/exemptions (resale, state food exemption, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus deductions — the base for the 2.9% state rate.

lines.label: State tax (2.9%)
lines.notes: Colorado state rate is 2.9%. State-collected county/city/special-district taxes are reported per jurisdiction on DR 0100; self-collecting home-rule cities (Denver, Boulder, Colorado Springs, etc.) file their own separate returns. TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Colorado jurisdiction.

notes: CO Form DR 0100 reports the 2.9% state rate plus state-collected local taxes per jurisdiction. Colorado has ~70 self-collecting home-rule cities that administer and file their own sales tax separately from the state return — those are out of scope for v1, which reports the state portion only. Groceries are exempt from the state tax but many home-rule cities tax them.

form_title: Connecticut Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross receipts
lines.notes: Total gross receipts from sales, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Deductions
lines.notes: Total deductions/exempt receipts (resale, exempt food, nonprescription drugs, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Taxable receipts
lines.notes: Gross minus deductions — the base for the 6.35% rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (6.35%)
lines.notes: Connecticut's general rate is 6.35% with no local sales tax, so this is the tax due for nearly all filers. Special rates apply separately: 7.75% on luxury goods (single items over $1,000), 1% on computer and data processing services (SaaS), and 7.35% on meals — adjust manually if those apply.

notes: CT Form OS-114 reports the 6.35% general rate. Connecticut has no general local sales tax, so the state line equals the tax due for nearly all filers. Special rates (7.75% luxury, 1% computer/data-processing/SaaS, 7.35% meals) are reported on separate lines/schedules and are not split by v1.

form_title: District of Columbia Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, exempt food/groceries, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 6.0% general rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: The District of Columbia general rate is 6.0% with no local sub-jurisdiction, so this is the tax due for most filers. Special DC rates apply separately and are not modeled here: 10% restaurant meals and off-premises alcohol, 10.25% on-premises alcohol and rental vehicles, 18% commercial parking.

notes: DC Form FR-800 reports the 6.0% general rate. The District has no local sub-jurisdiction, so the general line is the tax due for most filers. Special rates (10% meals/off-premises alcohol, 10.25% on-premises alcohol and rental cars, 18% parking) are reported on separate lines and not split by v1. DC exempts groceries and prescription drugs; DC taxes SaaS and digital goods.

form_title: Florida Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales (Sales/Services/Electricity)
lines.notes: Row A column 1 — Sales/Services/Electricity gross. Florida uses a per-category matrix (rows A-E) on the return; TaxLocus's v1 reports total gross under row A. Reclassify commercial-rental (C), transient-rental (D), and vending (E) amounts before filing if applicable.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Row A column 2 — exempt portion of gross. Includes resale, government, and other exempt categories.

lines.label: Taxable amount
lines.notes: Row A column 3 — line A1 minus line A2.

lines.label: Tax due (6.0%)
lines.notes: Row A column 4 — Florida state rate is 6.0%. County discretionary sales surtax (0.5%–2.5% depending on county) is added on the DR-15DSS surtax line and varies per delivery county — TaxLocus does not yet compute the per-county surtax split here.

lines.label: Total amount of tax due
lines.notes: Sum of column 4 across all rows (A-E). With only row A populated in v1, equals A4.

lines.label: Less lawful deductions
lines.notes: Lawful deductions (bad debts, returns, etc.) the merchant tracks separately. Defaults to 0; override if claiming.

lines.label: Net tax due
lines.notes: Line 5 minus line 6. With line 6 defaulted to 0, equals line 5.

lines.label: Less collection allowance (e-file/e-pay only)
lines.notes: 2.5% of the first $1,200 of tax due (max $30) for timely e-filed returns. v1 approximates as 2.5% of net tax due (0.0025 × 0.06 = 0.00015 of taxable sales); manually cap at $30 before filing. Forfeited if late or paper-filed.

notes: FL DR-15 has a per-category matrix (rows A through E: sales/services/electricity, taxable purchases, commercial rentals, transient rentals, food & beverage vending). v1 maps the full gross/exempt/taxable to row A; merchants with rental or vending revenue must reclassify before filing. The discretionary county surtax (DR-15DSS schedule) varies by delivery county (0.5–2.5%) and is not yet computed per-row — add the surtax separately if your delivery counties impose one.

form_title: Georgia Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Total state sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales. Whole dollars.

lines.label: Total exempt state sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, exempt organizations, state grocery exemption, prescription drugs, etc.). Georgia exempts groceries from the 4% state tax but local jurisdictions may still tax them — see the per-county note on the state-tax line.

lines.label: Taxable state sales
lines.notes: Line 1 minus line 2 — the base for the 4% state tax.

lines.label: State tax (4.0%)
lines.notes: Georgia state rate is 4.0%. The local taxes (LOST, SPLOST, ELOST, TSPLOST, MOST, and the local education tax) are computed per-county on the Part B Sales and Use Tax Distribution Table at each county's combined local rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Georgia county, so complete Part B from your county-level sales. Note: groceries are exempt from this 4% state tax but remain subject to local tax in most counties (O.C.G.A. § 48-8-3(57)).

lines.label: Vendor's compensation (3% / 0.5%, timely only)
lines.notes: Georgia vendor's compensation is 3% of the first $3,000 of combined state + local tax due plus 0.5% of the amount above $3,000, allowed only if filed and paid on time. This line approximates 3% of the state-portion tax (3% × 4% = 0.12% of taxable sales); recompute on the full combined tax and apply the $3,000 breakpoint by hand once the Part B local totals are in. Forfeited if late.

notes: GA ST-3 is filed through the Georgia Tax Center (monthly/quarterly/annual by volume). State rate is 4.0%; local taxes (LOST/SPLOST/ELOST/TSPLOST/MOST + education) are allocated per-county on the Part B distribution table at each county's combined local rate — TaxLocus v1 reports the 4.0% state portion only and does not split taxable sales by county. Groceries are exempt from the 4% state tax but generally remain locally taxable. Vendor's compensation (3% of the first $3,000 of combined tax, 0.5% above, timely filers only) is approximated on the state portion and must be recomputed on the combined tax with the $3,000 breakpoint applied by hand.

form_title: Hawaii General Excise/Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross income (retailing)
lines.notes: Total gross income from retailing, including marketplace-facilitated sales. Hawaii's GET applies broadly — groceries, services, and SaaS are all in the base.

lines.label: Exemptions/deductions
lines.notes: Total exemptions/deductions (resale at the wholesale rate, prescription drugs, etc.). Hawaii has very few exemptions — most receipts are taxable.

lines.label: Taxable income
lines.notes: Gross income minus exemptions — the base for the 4.0% GET.

lines.label: GET (4.0%)
lines.notes: Hawaii GET state rate is 4.0% for retailing. A 0.5% county surcharge applies on Oahu (Honolulu), Kauai, Hawaii, and Maui counties and is reported per county — TaxLocus v1 does not split by county. Note: GET is technically on the seller; many sellers pass it through at an effective 4.166%/4.712% to recover the tax-on-tax, but the return is filed on the 4.0%/4.5% rate.

notes: HI Form G-45 is the periodic General Excise/Use Tax Return. GET is a broad gross-income tax on the seller (retailing classification 4.0%); groceries, services, and SaaS are all taxable. A 0.5% county surcharge (Oahu, Kauai, Hawaii, Maui) applies on top and is reported per county — not split by v1. GET differs from a true sales tax; sellers commonly pass it through at 4.166% (4.712% with surcharge), but the return computes on the 4.0% base.

form_title: Iowa Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exemptions
lines.notes: Total exemptions (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exemptions — the base for the 6.0% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: Iowa state rate is 6.0%. A 1% Local Option Sales Tax (LOST) applies in most Iowa jurisdictions and is reported separately by jurisdiction — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Iowa locality.

notes: IA files the Sales and Use Tax Return through GovConnectIowa. State rate is 6.0%; a 1% Local Option Sales Tax applies in most jurisdictions and is reported per locality, not split by v1. Iowa exempts groceries; candy and soft drinks are taxable. SaaS is taxable with a B2B exemption for commercial enterprises.

form_title: Idaho Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Total sales
lines.notes: Total sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, prescription drugs, etc.). Idaho taxes groceries at the full rate (a grocery credit on the income-tax return offsets it).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Total sales minus exempt sales — the base for the 6.0% rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: Idaho state rate is 6.0%. Only a few resort cities and auditorium districts impose a local sales tax; those are reported separately and are not split by v1.

notes: ID Form 850 reports the 6.0% state rate, which is the tax due for nearly all filers — Idaho has no general local sales tax, only resort-city and auditorium-district local taxes in a handful of jurisdictions (not split by v1). Idaho taxes groceries at the full rate (offset by an income-tax grocery credit).

form_title: Illinois Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Total receipts
lines.notes: Step 1, Line 1 — total receipts (gross sales) for the month, including marketplace-facilitated sales. Whole dollars.

lines.label: Total deductions
lines.notes: Step 2, Line 4 — total deductions (resale, sales to exempt organizations, sales in interstate commerce, etc.). On the filed return these are itemized on Lines 4a–4f; TaxLocus reports the total exempt amount only.

lines.label: Taxable receipts
lines.notes: Step 3 — receipts subject to tax (Line 1 minus Line 4).

lines.label: General merchandise tax (6.25%)
lines.notes: Step 4 — general-merchandise base × 6.25% (the combined state ROT rate; the state retains 5.0% and remits 1.25% to local governments, but you report the full 6.25% here). Receipts from qualifying food, drugs, and medical appliances are taxed at 1.0% on a separate line (Line 6b / 7b) — TaxLocus v1 does not split the 1% food/drug base out of taxable receipts, so reclassify those before filing. Locally imposed home-rule and business-district taxes (Schedule GT / locations on Form ST-2) are also not computed here.

lines.label: Retailer's discount (1.75%, $5 min/month cap)
lines.notes: Illinois retailer's discount is 1.75% of the tax due if filed and paid on time (1.75% × 6.25% ≈ 0.109375% of taxable sales). The discount is subject to a $1,000/month aggregate cap across all returns — apply the cap by hand. Forfeited if late.

notes: IL ST-1 is monthly (quarterly/annual by volume). The 6.25% general-merchandise rate combines the 5.0% state and 1.25% statewide local ROT portions, reported together on Line 6a. v1 does NOT split the 1.0% food/drug/medical base (Line 6b/7b) out of taxable receipts, nor does it compute home-rule, county, or business-district taxes that ride on the locations listed on Form ST-2 / Schedule GT. The 1.75% retailer's discount is approximated on the state-portion tax and is subject to a $1,000/month aggregate cap to apply by hand.

form_title: Indiana Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Total sales
lines.notes: Total sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Total sales minus exempt sales — the base for the 7.0% rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (7.0%)
lines.notes: Indiana's rate is a flat 7.0% with no local sales tax, so this is the tax due for all filers.

notes: IN Form ST-103 reports the flat 7.0% rate. Indiana has no local sales tax, so the state line equals the tax due for all filers. Indiana exempts groceries; candy and soft drinks are taxable.

form_title: Kansas Retailers' Sales Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross receipts from sales, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Deductions
lines.notes: Total deductions/exempt sales (resale, prescription drugs, etc.). The state grocery tax phased to 0% in Jan 2025; local food tax continues.

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus deductions — the base for the 6.5% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (6.5%)
lines.notes: Kansas state rate is 6.5%. County and city taxes are reported per jurisdiction on the ST-36 at each jurisdiction's combined rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Kansas locality.

notes: KS Form ST-36 is the multi-jurisdiction Retailers' Sales Tax Return (ST-16 for a single jurisdiction). State rate is 6.5%; county/city taxes are reported per jurisdiction and not split by v1. The Kansas state sales tax on food fully phased out to 0% on Jan 1, 2025 (HB 2106); local food taxes continue.

form_title: Kentucky Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross receipts
lines.notes: Total gross receipts from sales, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exemptions/deductions
lines.notes: Total exemptions/deductions (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable receipts
lines.notes: Gross minus exemptions — the base for the 6.0% rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: Kentucky's rate is a flat 6.0% with no local sales tax, so this is the tax due for all filers.

notes: KY Form 51A102 reports the flat 6.0% rate. Kentucky has no local sales tax. Kentucky exempts groceries; candy and soft drinks are taxable. Kentucky taxes SaaS and many services after its 2023 expansion.

form_title: Louisiana Sales Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales of tangible personal property, services, and digital products, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Allowable deductions
lines.notes: Total allowable deductions/exempt sales (resale, food for home consumption (state), prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus deductions — the base for the 5.0% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (5.0%)
lines.notes: Louisiana state rate is 5.0% (raised from 4.45% effective Jan 1, 2025). Parish and local sales taxes (often 4-7%) are filed separately with the local collector — TaxLocus v1 reports the state portion only and does not split by parish.

notes: LA Form R-1029 reports the state sales tax. The state rate rose to 5.0% on Jan 1, 2025 (from 4.45%). Louisiana has high parish/local rates that are administered and filed separately with parish collectors — not split by v1. Louisiana began taxing digital products and SaaS on Jan 1, 2025 (Act 11, 2024). Food for home consumption is exempt from the state rate but locally taxable.

form_title: Massachusetts Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, clothing under $175 per item, food, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 6.25% rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (6.25%)
lines.notes: Massachusetts' rate is a flat 6.25% with no local sales tax. Meals carry an optional 0.75% local meals tax filed separately on Form ST-MAB — not modeled here.

notes: MA Form ST-9 reports the flat 6.25% rate. Massachusetts has no general local sales tax. Clothing is exempt up to $175 per item (only the excess is taxable); food and prescription medicine are exempt; candy and soft drinks are treated as food and exempt. Meals add an optional 0.75% local meals tax (Form ST-MAB), not modeled by v1.

form_title: Maryland Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, grocery food, medicine, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 6.0% rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: Maryland's general rate is 6.0% with no local sales tax. Alcoholic beverages are taxed at 9% on a separate line — not modeled here.

notes: MD Form 202 reports the 6.0% general rate. Maryland has no local sales tax. Alcoholic beverages are taxed at 9% (separate line, not modeled). Maryland taxes digital products and SaaS broadly (HB 932, 2021). Groceries are exempt; medicines (including many OTC) are exempt.

form_title: Maine Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, grocery staples, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 5.5% general rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (5.5%)
lines.notes: Maine's general rate is 5.5% with no local sales tax. Special rates apply separately and are not modeled here: prepared food 8%, lodging 9%, short-term auto rental 10%.

notes: ME files the Sales and Use Tax Return through the Maine Tax Portal. General rate is 5.5% with no local sales tax. Maine charges higher special rates on prepared food (8%), lodging (9%), and short-term auto rentals (10%) — not split by v1. Grocery staples are exempt; candy and soft drinks are taxable.

form_title: Michigan Sales, Use and Withholding Taxes Monthly/Quarterly Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period (sales-tax section of Form 5080), including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Allowable deductions (exempt sales)
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, exempt-org sales, etc.). On Form 5080 these are itemized on the deductions worksheet; TaxLocus reports the total exempt amount.

lines.label: Taxable balance
lines.notes: Gross sales minus allowable deductions — the base for the 6% tax.

lines.label: Sales tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: Michigan's flat 6.0% rate. There is no general local sales tax, so for nearly all filers this is the actual tax due.

lines.label: Timely-filing discount (approx.)
lines.notes: Michigan grants a discount for timely filing/payment, computed on a tiered, capped schedule per the Form 5080 instructions (commonly ~0.5% of tax with monthly maximums). This line approximates 0.5% of the 6% state tax (0.5% × 6% = 0.03% of taxable sales); recompute and cap per the official worksheet before filing. Forfeited if late.

notes: MI Form 5080 is the combined Sales, Use and Withholding monthly/quarterly return (Form 5081 is the annual reconciliation); only the sales-tax section is mapped. Michigan has a flat 6.0% rate and no general local sales tax, so the tax line equals the amount due for nearly all filers. The timely-filing discount uses a tiered, capped schedule (Form 5080 instructions) — approximated here as ~0.5% of tax and must be recomputed and capped by hand. Use tax on purchases and withholding are separate sections of the same form, out of scope for v1.

form_title: Minnesota Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross receipts
lines.notes: Total gross receipts for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt receipts
lines.notes: Total exempt receipts (resale, clothing, food, prescription drugs, etc.). Minnesota exempts clothing statewide.

lines.label: Taxable receipts
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 6.875% state rate.

lines.label: State general rate tax (6.875%)
lines.notes: Minnesota state rate is 6.875%. Local taxes (city/county/transit) are reported per jurisdiction in e-Services at each jurisdiction's rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Minnesota locality.

notes: MN files the Sales and Use Tax Return through Minnesota e-Services. State rate is 6.875%; local taxes are reported per jurisdiction and not split by v1. Minnesota exempts clothing, food, and prescription drugs; candy and soft drinks are taxable. SaaS is exempt; specified digital products are taxable.

form_title: Missouri Sales Tax Return

lines.label: Gross receipts
lines.notes: Total gross receipts for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Adjustments/exemptions
lines.notes: Total exemptions/adjustments (resale, prescription drugs, etc.). Food for home consumption is taxed at the reduced 1.225% state rate, reported separately.

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exemptions — the base for the 4.225% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (4.225%)
lines.notes: Missouri state rate is 4.225%. County, city, and special-district taxes are reported per location on Form 53-1 at each location's combined rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Missouri locality.

notes: MO Form 53-1 reports the 4.225% state rate. County/city/district taxes are reported per location and not split by v1. Food for home consumption is taxed at the reduced 1.225% state rate (separate line). Missouri does not tax SaaS or digital products.

form_title: Mississippi Sales Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales/proceeds for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, prescription drugs, etc.). Mississippi taxes groceries (reduced to 5% effective July 2025); reported on a separate line at the reduced rate.

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 7.0% rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (7.0%)
lines.notes: Mississippi's general rate is 7.0%. Local sales taxes are limited to a few cities (tourism/special levies) and are reported separately — not split by v1.

notes: MS Form 72-010 reports the 7.0% general rate, which is the tax due for nearly all filers — Mississippi has very limited local sales tax. Groceries are taxed (reduced from 7% to 5% effective July 1, 2025) and reported at the reduced rate on a separate line; candy and soft drinks are at the full rate. Mississippi taxes computer software services (SaaS).

form_title: North Carolina Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: North Carolina gross receipts
lines.notes: Total North Carolina gross receipts for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales. Whole dollars.

lines.label: Exempt and nontaxable receipts
lines.notes: Total exempt/nontaxable receipts (resale, exempt food from the State rate, prescription drugs, exempt-org sales, etc.).

lines.label: Receipts subject to the General State rate
lines.notes: Gross receipts less exempt receipts — the base for the 4.75% General State rate.

lines.label: General State rate tax (4.75%)
lines.notes: North Carolina's General State rate is 4.75%. The county 2.00% local tax plus any applicable transit tax (0.25%/0.50%) is computed per county on the E-500 county breakdown at each county's combined local rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by NC county, so complete the county lines from your county-level sales. Groceries are exempt from this 4.75% State rate but subject to the separate 2% local food tax.

notes: NC Form E-500 is the Sales and Use Tax Return (monthly/quarterly by volume). General State rate is 4.75%; the county 2.00% local tax plus transit taxes (0.25%/0.50% in some counties) are reported per county on the E-500 breakdown, and v1 reports the 4.75% State portion only — complete the county lines from your county-level sales. Groceries are exempt from the State rate but subject to the 2% local food tax (a separate line). Prepared food is taxed at the full combined rate. North Carolina does not offer a timely-filing vendor discount.

form_title: North Dakota Sales, Use, and Gross Receipts Tax Return

lines.label: Total sales
lines.notes: Total sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Nontaxable sales
lines.notes: Total nontaxable/exempt sales (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.). North Dakota exempts groceries.

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Total minus nontaxable — the base for the 5.0% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (5.0%)
lines.notes: North Dakota state rate is 5.0%. City and county local taxes are reported per jurisdiction on the local schedule — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by North Dakota locality.

notes: ND files the Sales, Use, and Gross Receipts Tax Return (Form ST). State rate is 5.0%; city/county local taxes are reported per jurisdiction and not split by v1. North Dakota exempts groceries; candy and soft drinks are taxable. Prewritten software is taxable but other digital goods are not.

form_title: Nebraska and Local Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.). Nebraska exempts groceries.

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 5.5% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (5.5%)
lines.notes: Nebraska state rate is 5.5%. City and county local taxes (up to 2%) are reported per jurisdiction on Form 10 — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Nebraska locality.

notes: NE Form 10 reports the 5.5% state rate plus local taxes per jurisdiction (not split by v1). Nebraska exempts groceries; candy and soft drinks are taxable. SaaS is exempt.

form_title: New Jersey Sales and Use Tax Quarterly Return

lines.label: Gross receipts
lines.notes: Total gross receipts for the quarter, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Deductions (exempt receipts)
lines.notes: Total exempt receipts (resale, exempt clothing/footwear, groceries, prescription drugs, exempt-org sales, etc.). New Jersey broadly exempts clothing and most groceries — verify your taxability mapping reflects those exemptions.

lines.label: Receipts subject to Sales Tax
lines.notes: Line 1 minus line 2 — the base for the 6.625% tax.

lines.label: Sales Tax due (6.625%)
lines.notes: New Jersey's single statewide rate is 6.625% — there is no general local sales tax, so for most filers this is the actual tax due. Two exceptions TaxLocus v1 does not split out: certified Urban Enterprise Zone (UEZ) businesses charge half the rate (3.3125%) on qualifying in-zone retail sales, and Salem County applies a 3.5% rate. Adjust manually if either applies to your sales. NJ also has no timely-filing vendor discount.

notes: NJ ST-50 is the quarterly return; ST-51 is the monthly remittance for the first two months of a quarter. New Jersey has a single statewide 6.625% rate and no general local sales tax, so the state-portion line is the actual tax due for nearly all filers. Narrow exceptions not computed in v1: Urban Enterprise Zone certified businesses charge a half rate (3.3125%) on qualifying in-zone sales, and Salem County uses a 3.5% rate. New Jersey offers no vendor/timely-filing discount. NJ broadly exempts clothing and footwear and most groceries — confirm the taxability mapping covers these before relying on the deductions figure.

form_title: New Mexico Gross Receipts Tax Return

lines.label: Gross receipts
lines.notes: Total gross receipts for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales. GRT is broad — services and SaaS are in the base.

lines.label: Deductions
lines.notes: Total deductions (resale, food (grocery), prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Taxable gross receipts
lines.notes: Gross minus deductions — the base for the 4.875% state GRT.

lines.label: State GRT (4.875%)
lines.notes: New Mexico's state GRT rate is 4.875%. Local-option GRT increments are reported per location/business-location code at each location's combined rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split by NM location code.

notes: NM Form TRD-41413 reports the 4.875% state Gross Receipts Tax. GRT is a broad gross-receipts tax on the seller (services and SaaS are taxable). Local-option increments are reported per business-location code and not split by v1. Food (grocery) and prescription drugs are deductible.

form_title: Nevada Combined Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Total sales
lines.notes: Total sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.). Nevada exempts groceries.

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Total sales minus exempt — the base for tax.

lines.label: State-imposed tax (4.6%)
lines.notes: Nevada's state-imposed components are 2.0% Sales Tax + 2.6% Local School Support Tax = 4.6%. Additional mandatory and optional county taxes bring every county to at least a 6.85% combined minimum (Clark County is 8.375%). The return is filed per county at the county's combined rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Nevada county, so use the county's full combined rate when filing.

notes: NV Form TXR-01.01 is the Combined Sales and Use Tax Return, filed per county. The state-imposed rate (2.0% Sales Tax + 2.6% LSST) is 4.6%; mandatory + optional county taxes bring every county to at least 6.85% combined. v1 reports the 4.6% state portion only and does not split by county — use the destination county's full combined rate for the actual tax due. Nevada exempts groceries and does not tax SaaS or digital products.

form_title: New York State and Local Quarterly Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales and services
lines.notes: Step 1 — total gross sales and services for the quarter, including marketplace-facilitated sales. Whole dollars.

lines.label: Sales subject to tax
lines.notes: Gross less exempt (resale, exempt-organization, exempt food, clothing under $110, etc.). On the filed return this total is the sum of the per-jurisdiction taxable amounts entered on the ST-100 jurisdiction lines / Schedules A, B, H, etc.

lines.label: State portion (4.0%)
lines.notes: Informational only. New York's 4.0% state rate × taxable sales. NY does NOT report a standalone state line on ST-100 — each jurisdiction line on the return applies the combined state + local + MCTD rate for that jurisdiction. Use this as a reconciliation check; the actual tax due is the sum of the jurisdiction schedules, which TaxLocus does not yet compute per-jurisdiction.

lines.label: Vendor collection credit (5%, max $200)
lines.notes: Vendor collection credit is 5% of the tax due (5% × 4% state ≈ 0.2% of taxable sales as a state-portion approximation), capped at $200 per quarter and only if filed and paid on time. Cap by hand. The credit is computed on total tax due (state + local), so the true figure will be higher than this state-only approximation once the jurisdiction schedules are completed.

notes: NY ST-100 is quarterly and jurisdiction-schedule driven: every taxable line on the return carries the combined state (4.0%) + local county/city + MCTD rate for that jurisdiction, entered on the ST-100 jurisdiction lines and Schedules A/B/H/etc. TaxLocus v1 reports gross, taxable, and an informational 4.0% state-portion figure; it does not yet split taxable sales across the ~80 NY reporting jurisdictions, so the per-jurisdiction lines and the final tax due must be completed from the schedules. The 5% vendor collection credit (max $200/quarter) is approximated on the state portion only and must be capped by hand.

form_title: Ohio Universal Sales Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Line 1 — total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Line 2 — exempt sales (resale, sales to exempt entities, exempt food for off-premises consumption, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Line 3 — reportable taxable sales (Line 1 minus Line 2).

lines.label: Tax liability (state portion, 5.75%)
lines.notes: Ohio is destination-based: the tax actually due is the combined state (5.75%) + county (and transit-authority) rate at each buyer's address, broken out by county on Schedule UST-1C. This line shows the 5.75% state portion only. Filers MUST complete UST-1C to allocate taxable sales across counties — the county tax will increase the total above this state-only figure. TaxLocus v1 does not yet split taxable sales by Ohio county.

lines.label: Discount (0.75%, max $750/period)
lines.notes: Ohio's timely-filing discount is 0.75% of the tax liability (0.75% × 5.75% ≈ 0.0431% of taxable sales), capped at $750 per return period and only if filed and paid on time. Cap by hand. The discount applies to total tax (state + county), so the figure will be higher once UST-1C county allocations are added.

notes: OH UST-1 is filed semi-monthly or monthly through the Ohio Business Gateway. Ohio sales tax is destination-based: the combined state (5.75%) + county + transit-authority rate at the buyer's address, allocated per county on Schedule UST-1C. v1 reports gross, exempt, taxable, and the 5.75% state portion only — the per-county allocation on UST-1C must be completed before filing, and it raises the total tax above the state-only figure shown here. The 0.75% discount (max $750/period) is approximated on the state portion and must be capped by hand.

form_title: Oklahoma Sales Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt/deductible sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, groceries (state-exempt since Aug 2024), prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 4.5% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (4.5%)
lines.notes: Oklahoma state rate is 4.5%. County and city taxes are reported per jurisdiction at each jurisdiction's combined rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Oklahoma locality.

notes: OK Form STS-20002 reports the 4.5% state rate. County/city taxes are reported per jurisdiction and not split by v1. Oklahoma eliminated the state sales tax on groceries effective Aug 2024 (HB 1955); local sales taxes on groceries continue. Oklahoma does not tax SaaS or digital products.

form_title: Pennsylvania Sales, Use, and Hotel Occupancy Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross less exempt (resale, clothing, most groceries, prescription drugs, and other PA exemptions). Pennsylvania exempts clothing and most food, so confirm your taxability mapping covers these.

lines.label: State tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: Pennsylvania state rate is 6.0%. Multiply net taxable sales by 0.06.

lines.label: Allegheny County local tax (1.0%)
lines.notes: Allegheny County imposes an additional 1.0% local sales tax on sales sourced there. Defaults to 0 — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by delivery county. If you have Allegheny-sourced sales, compute 1.0% of that portion and enter it here.

lines.label: Philadelphia local tax (2.0%)
lines.notes: Philadelphia imposes an additional 2.0% local sales tax on sales sourced there. Defaults to 0 — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by delivery jurisdiction. If you have Philadelphia-sourced sales, compute 2.0% of that portion and enter it here.

lines.label: Vendor discount (1%, capped)
lines.notes: Pennsylvania's vendor discount is 1% of tax collected (1% × 6% = 0.06% of taxable sales), capped at $25 per monthly return or $75 per quarterly/semi-annual return, and only if filed and paid on time. Cap by hand. The discount applies to total tax collected, so include any Allegheny/Philadelphia tax before applying the cap.

notes: PA-3 is filed through myPATH (monthly/quarterly/semi-annual by volume). State rate is 6.0%; Allegheny County adds 1.0% and Philadelphia adds 2.0% on sales sourced to those jurisdictions — both default to 0 here because TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by delivery county/city. The 1% vendor discount is capped ($25/month, $75/quarter) and must be applied by hand. Pennsylvania broadly exempts clothing and most groceries — verify your taxability mapping reflects these before relying on the net-taxable figure.

form_title: Rhode Island Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, clothing under $250 per item, food, prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 7.0% rate.

lines.label: Sales tax (7.0%)
lines.notes: Rhode Island's rate is a flat 7.0% with no local sales tax, so this is the tax due for all filers.

notes: RI Form T-204 reports the flat 7.0% rate. Rhode Island has no local sales tax. Clothing is exempt up to $250 per item (only the excess is taxable); food and prescription drugs are exempt; candy and soft drinks are taxable. SaaS (vendor-hosted prewritten software) is taxable.

form_title: South Carolina State Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross proceeds of sales
lines.notes: Total gross proceeds of sales, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Deductions
lines.notes: Total deductions/exempt sales (resale, unprepared food (state-exempt), prescription drugs, etc.).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus deductions — the base for the 6.0% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: South Carolina state rate is 6.0%. Local option taxes (typically 1-2%) are reported per county on the ST-389 schedule — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by South Carolina county.

notes: SC Form ST-3 reports the 6.0% state rate; local option taxes are reported per county on the ST-389 schedule and not split by v1. Unprepared food is exempt from the state rate (local may tax). SaaS and streaming are taxable as communications services.

form_title: South Dakota Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales. South Dakota has a broad base — services and groceries are taxable.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, prescription drugs, etc.). Note: South Dakota taxes groceries (no food exemption).

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 4.2% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (4.2%)
lines.notes: South Dakota state rate is 4.2% (reduced from 4.5% through 2027). Municipal taxes (up to 2%) are reported per jurisdiction — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by South Dakota municipality.

notes: SD files the Sales and Use Tax Return through SD EPath. State rate is 4.2% (a temporary reduction from 4.5%, scheduled to expire after 2026). Municipal taxes are reported per jurisdiction and not split by v1. South Dakota has a broad base — services, SaaS, and groceries are all taxable (the IM 28 grocery exemption failed in Nov 2024).

form_title: Tennessee Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, prescription drugs, etc.). Food for home consumption is taxed at the reduced 4.0% state rate, reported separately.

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 7.0% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (7.0%)
lines.notes: Tennessee state rate is 7.0%. Local option taxes (2.25%-2.75%, capped on the first $1,600 per single article) are reported per jurisdiction on Form SLS-450 — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Tennessee locality.

notes: TN Form SLS-450 reports the 7.0% state rate. Local option taxes (2.25%-2.75%) are reported per jurisdiction and not split by v1; the local single-article cap ($1,600) is also not applied. Food for home consumption is taxed at the reduced 4.0% state rate (separate line); candy, dietary supplements, and prepared food are at the full rate. SaaS and specified digital products are taxable.

form_title: Texas Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Total Texas sales
lines.notes: Whole dollars only. Includes marketplace-facilitated sales — the marketplace facilitator remits the tax but the sale still flows on line 1 (see Form 01-922 instructions). Subtract marketplace sales on Form 01-148 if claiming a credit.

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Whole dollars only. Gross sales less exempt sales (resale, agricultural, government, etc.).

lines.label: Taxable purchases
lines.notes: Use-tax purchases TaxLocus cannot infer from the sales ledger — your own purchase records. Defaults to 0; override before filing if you owe use tax on out-of-state purchases.

lines.label: Amount subject to state tax
lines.notes: Item 2 plus Item 3. With line 3 defaulted to 0, this equals taxable sales.

lines.label: State tax due (6.25%)
lines.notes: State rate is 6.25% (0.062500). Multiply line 4 by 0.0625 — Texas does not allow rounding the rate.

lines.label: Total state tax (sum of outlets)
lines.notes: Sum of line 7a across all outlets. Single-location filers: equal to line 7a.

lines.label: Timely filing discount (0.5%)
lines.notes: 0.5% of state tax (line 10) if filed and paid by the due date. 0.5% × 6.25% rate = 0.003125% of taxable sales. Forfeited if late.

notes: Texas marketplace-facilitator rule: marketplace-facilitated sales flow through line 1 (gross) but the facilitator remits the tax; claim credit via Form 01-148. Local tax (line 5/7b/8b) is omitted from v1 — TaxLocus does not yet aggregate local-rate splits per transaction (see plan 05 v2). Single state-rate filers cover the vast majority of TX returns.

form_title: Utah Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Total sales
lines.notes: Total sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, prescription drugs, etc.). Grocery food is taxed at the reduced 3.0% combined rate, reported separately.

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Total minus exempt — the base for the 4.85% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (4.85%)
lines.notes: Utah state rate is 4.85%. Uniform local (1.0%), county (0.25%), and additional local taxes bring the combined statewide minimum to ~6.1%; these are reported per jurisdiction on Form TC-62S — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Utah locality.

notes: UT Form TC-62S reports the 4.85% state rate. Uniform and county/city local taxes bring the combined statewide minimum to ~6.1% and are reported per jurisdiction (not split by v1). Grocery food is taxed at the reduced 3.0% combined rate (separate line); candy, soft drinks, and prepared food are at the full rate. SaaS is exempt; products transferred electronically are taxable.

form_title: Virginia Retail Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales and/or rentals for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, exempt-org, prescription drugs, etc.). Virginia exempts most groceries from the state rate (taxed at the 1% reduced food rate, reported separately).

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross sales minus exempt sales — the base for the state and local tax.

lines.label: State tax (4.3%)
lines.notes: Virginia state rate is 4.3%.

lines.label: Local tax (1.0%)
lines.notes: Uniform 1.0% statewide local tax. Combined state + local minimum is 5.3%. Regional add-on taxes (Hampton Roads, Northern Virginia, Central Virginia, etc., +0.7% to +1.0%) apply on top in those regions and are reported separately — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by region.

notes: VA Form ST-9 is the Retail Sales and Use Tax Return. The state rate is 4.3% plus a uniform 1.0% statewide local tax (5.3% combined minimum), both modeled here. Regional add-on transportation taxes (Hampton Roads, Northern Virginia, Central Virginia, etc.) apply in those regions on top of the 5.3% and are not split by v1. Virginia eliminated the state tax on groceries in 2023; groceries and essential personal hygiene products are taxed at the 1% reduced rate, reported separately. No timely-filing dealer discount is modeled (Virginia's dealer discount is volume-tiered and phased out for larger filers).

form_title: Vermont Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, clothing, food, prescription drugs, etc.). Vermont exempts clothing and food.

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 6.0% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: Vermont state rate is 6.0%. A 1% local option tax applies in some municipalities and is reported separately — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Vermont municipality.

notes: VT Form SUT-451 reports the 6.0% state rate. A 1% local option tax applies in certain municipalities and is reported separately (not split by v1). Vermont exempts clothing and food; soft drinks are taxable but candy is treated as food and exempt. SaaS is exempt; specified digital products are taxable.

form_title: Washington Combined Excise Tax Return — Retail Sales Tax

lines.label: Gross amount (retail sales)
lines.notes: Gross amount of retail sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales. Reported in the Retail Sales Tax section of the Combined Excise Tax Return.

lines.label: Deductions (exempt sales)
lines.notes: Total deductions/exempt sales (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.). On the CETR these are itemized on the Deduction Detail page; TaxLocus reports the total exempt amount.

lines.label: Taxable amount
lines.notes: Gross amount minus deductions — the base for the 6.5% state retail sales tax.

lines.label: State retail sales tax (6.5%)
lines.notes: Washington's state retail sales tax rate is 6.5%. The local sales tax is reported separately, per location code, on the Local Sales & Use Tax addendum at each location's combined local rate — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by WA location code, so the local addendum must be completed from your location-level sales. The state-portion figure here will be lower than the total tax once local is added.

notes: WA files the Combined Excise Tax Return (CETR), which bundles Business & Occupation (B&O) tax, Retail Sales Tax, and Use Tax; only the retail-sales-tax section is mapped. Washington is destination-based: the local sales tax is reported per location code on the Local Sales & Use Tax addendum at each location's combined local rate, and v1 reports the 6.5% state portion only — complete the local addendum from your location-level sales. The B&O gross-receipts tax is a separate computation on the same return and is out of scope. Washington offers no timely-filing vendor discount for sales tax.

form_title: Wisconsin Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Total sales
lines.notes: Total sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.). Wisconsin exempts groceries.

lines.label: Taxable sales
lines.notes: Total minus exempt — the base for the 5.0% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (5.0%)
lines.notes: Wisconsin state rate is 5.0%. County (0.5%) and special-district taxes (e.g. the Milwaukee local taxes) are reported per county on the ST-12 — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Wisconsin county.

notes: WI Form ST-12 reports the 5.0% state rate. County (0.5%) and special-district taxes are reported per county and not split by v1. Wisconsin exempts groceries; candy and soft drinks are taxable. SaaS is exempt; prewritten software and specified digital goods are taxable.

form_title: West Virginia Combined Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exemptions/deductions
lines.notes: Total exemptions/deductions (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.). West Virginia exempts groceries.

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exemptions — the base for the 6.0% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (6.0%)
lines.notes: West Virginia state rate is 6.0%. Municipal sales taxes (typically 1%) apply in many cities and are reported per municipality on the CST-200CU — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by West Virginia municipality.

notes: WV Form CST-200CU is the Combined Sales and Use Tax Return. State rate is 6.0%; municipal sales taxes (typically 1%) are reported per municipality and not split by v1. West Virginia exempts groceries; prepared food is taxable.

form_title: Wyoming Sales and Use Tax Return

lines.label: Gross sales
lines.notes: Total gross sales for the period, including marketplace-facilitated sales.

lines.label: Exempt sales
lines.notes: Total exempt sales (resale, exempt food, prescription drugs, etc.). Wyoming exempts groceries.

lines.label: Net taxable sales
lines.notes: Gross minus exempt — the base for the 4.0% state rate.

lines.label: State sales tax (4.0%)
lines.notes: Wyoming state rate is 4.0%. County option taxes (typically 1-2%) are reported per county — TaxLocus v1 does not split taxable sales by Wyoming county.

notes: WY files the Sales and Use Tax Return with the Excise Tax Division. State rate is 4.0%; county option taxes (typically 1-2%) are reported per county and not split by v1. Wyoming exempts groceries; prepared food is taxable. Wyoming does not tax SaaS.
